steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To identify the three primary earthing system types (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
steps.retrievalText:
Lesson: Earthing Systems and ADS Components
To identify the three primary earthing system types (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
1. TT earthing system
2. TN-S earthing system
3. TN-C-S earthing system
We will work in short chunks: teaching first, then checks, then example, practice, and a final integrative task.

steps.title: Earthing System Types
steps.objective: Identify and distinguish between TT, TN-S, and TN-C-S earthing systems.
steps.retrievalText:
Earthing systems are identified by a coding system where 'T' stands for Terre (Earth), 'N' for Neutral, 'S' for Separate, and 'C' for Combined. In a TT earthing system, the supplier provides no earth terminal. Instead, the installation relies on a local earth electrode, such as a copper-plated rod driven into the ground, to provide the connection to Earth. This means the 'Terra-Terra' arrangement uses the soil itself as the return path for fault current.
In a TN-S earthing system, the supplier provides a dedicated, separate earth conductor all the way from the transformer to the consumer's intake. This is often the lead sheath or a separate wire within the supply cable. The neutral and earth conductors are never connected together within the installation.
A TN-C-S earthing system uses a single conductor for both neutral and earth in the supplier's street cables (combined), but these are separated into two distinct terminals at the consumer's main cut-out. This arrangement is the most common for new domestic properties and is frequently referred to as Protective Multiple Earthing (PME).

steps.title: ADS Component Parts
steps.objective: Identify the specific components that make up an ADS system.
steps.retrievalText:
Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) relies on several specific components working together to clear a fault safely. The earthing conductor is the heavy-duty green and yellow cable that connects the installation's Main Earth Terminal to the means of earthing, such as the supplier's terminal or an earth electrode. Do not confuse this with the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC), which is the individual earth wire found within each specific circuit, such as the central wire in a twin and earth cable.
Main protective bonding conductors are used to connect metallic services like incoming gas and water pipes to the main earth terminal. This ensures all accessible metalwork stays at the same potential, reducing shock risk. In high-risk areas like bathrooms, supplementary bonding may be used to link metal parts together locally. Finally, the protective device, such as an MCB or fuse, is the component that actually disconnects the power. It detects the high current flowing through the CPC during a fault and 'trips' or blows to stop the flow of electricity instantly.

answerGuidance: Ensures metalwork stays at the same potential

steps.title: Worked Example
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to identify the earthing system and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic installation using a PME (TN-C-S) supply.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are inspecting the service intake of a modern semi-detached house. You observe that the supplier's incoming cable enters a cut-out where the earth terminal is physically linked to the neutral block. From this point, a single green and yellow cable runs to the consumer unit's Main Earth Terminal (MET).
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Look at the relationship between Neutral and Earth at the intake. Since they are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the cut-out into distinct terminals, this is a TN-C-S system (also known as PME).
Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. Locate the heavy-duty green and yellow cable connecting the supplier’s earth terminal to the MET. This is the Earthing Conductor, the primary link for the entire installation to the earth source.
Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). Look at the internal wiring of a socket circuit. The green and yellow wire within the twin and earth cable is the CPC. Its job is to carry fault current from that specific circuit back to the MET.
Step 4: Identify the Protective Devices. Look at the consumer unit. The MCBs (Miniature Circuit Breakers) are the protective devices. When a fault occurs and current flows through the CPC, the MCB detects the surge and trips.
Step 5: Identify Main Protective Bonding. Locate the green and yellow cables running to the incoming metallic gas and water pipes. These are the Main Protective Bonding conductors, required to keep all metalwork at the same potential.
Final Result: The installation is a TN-C-S system. The ADS components identified are the Earthing Conductor, CPCs, Protective Devices (MCBs), and Main Protective Bonding conductors.
Takeaway: ADS requires a complete path and a switching device. The CPC and Earthing Conductor provide the path for fault current, while the Protective Device (MCB) provides the automatic disconnection.
steps.hint: Remember to distinguish between the conductor that serves the whole house (Earthing Conductor) and the one that serves just one circuit (CPC).

steps.title: Guided Practice
steps.objective: Identify the earthing system and ADS components for a rural installation using a local earth rod.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are working at a rural cottage. The electricity supplier has not provided an earth terminal. To provide an earth path, a copper-plated rod has been driven into the soil outside.
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the installation relies on a local connection to the earth (Terra) and the source is also earthed (Terra), this is a TT system.
Step 2: Identify the Earth Electrode. The copper-plated rod driven into the ground is the Earth Electrode.
Step 3: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The green and yellow cable connecting the Earth Electrode to the Main Earth Terminal (MET) is the Earthing Conductor.
Step 4: Identify the Protective Device. In a TT system, an RCD is typically used as the protective device to ensure automatic disconnection because the earth path through the soil has higher resistance.
steps.questionText: Based on the scenario, name the earthing system type, the component driven into the ground, and the specific conductor that links that component to the consumer unit.
steps.hint: In a TT system, the 'T' stands for 'Terre' (Earth), meaning both the source and the installation have their own independent connections to the ground.

steps.title: Practice
steps.objective: Correctly identify a TN-S system and its specific ADS components from a technical description.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are inspecting a large Victorian house. The incoming supply cable has a dedicated metal sheath that acts as the earth return path. This earth path is kept entirely separate from the neutral conductor from the transformer all the way to the consumer's installation.
Task: You must identify the system type and the specific conductors used for a single shower circuit within this installation.
steps.questionText: 1. Identify the earthing system type described. 2. Name the conductor that connects the metal casing of the shower unit to the consumer unit's earth bar. 3. Name the conductor that connects the consumer unit's earth bar to the supplier's dedicated earth terminal.
steps.hint: Think about the 'S' in the acronym—it stands for 'Separate'. Also, distinguish between the conductor for a single circuit and the conductor for the whole installation.

steps.title: Integrative Close
steps.objective: Synthesize the identification of a TN-C-S system with the correct application of specific ADS conductors for circuits and services.
steps.retrievalText: To master earthing and ADS, you must be able to look at a supply arrangement and immediately know which components are required to make it safe. In a TN-C-S (PME) system, the supply arrives with a combined neutral and earth, but the internal installation must treat them as separate. Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is only achieved when the correct conductors—the Earthing Conductor, the CPC, and the Main Protective Bonding—are all installed to provide a low-resistance path for fault current and to equalize potential across metal services.
steps.questionText: You are installing a new consumer unit in a property with a TN-C-S (PME) supply. To ensure the ADS system is compliant, you must connect the installation to the supplier's terminal, wire a new lighting circuit, and secure the incoming metallic water main. Identify the earthing system type and name the three specific green-and-yellow conductors required for these three distinct tasks.
answerGuidance: Earthing Conductor (to connect the MET to the supplier's terminal)
Circuit Protective Conductor / CPC (for the lighting circuit)
Main Protective Bonding Conductor (for the metallic water main)
steps.hint: Think about the specific names we use for conductors based on their job: one connects the whole house to the source, one protects an individual circuit, and one 'bonds' the metal pipes.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To identify the three primary earthing system types (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
steps.retrievalText: Every electrical installation needs a safety net. If a fault occurs and a metal appliance becomes live, the earthing system provides a safe path for that current, and the ADS (Automatic Disconnection of Supply) acts as the emergency off-switch. Today we will learn how to identify the three main types of earthing systems (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and the components that make this safety net work. By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to identify these systems and the specific conductors required to keep an installation safe. We will work in short chunks: teaching first, then checks, then a worked example, practice, and a final integrative task.

steps.title: Earthing System Types
steps.objective: Identify and distinguish between TT, TN-S, and TN-C-S earthing systems.
steps.retrievalText:
Earthing systems are identified by a coding system: 'T' stands for Terre (Earth), 'N' for Neutral, 'S' for Separate, and 'C' for Combined. 

In a **TT** system, the supplier provides no earth terminal. The installation relies on a local earth electrode (like a copper rod) driven into the ground. The soil itself is the return path.

In a **TN-S** system, the supplier provides a dedicated, separate earth conductor from the transformer to the consumer. This is often the lead sheath of the supply cable. Neutral and earth are separate throughout.

In a **TN-C-S** system, the supplier uses one conductor for both neutral and earth in the street cables (combined), but they are separated into two distinct terminals at the consumer's main cut-out. This is the most common domestic arrangement, often called PME (Protective Multiple Earthing).

steps.title: ADS Component Parts
steps.objective: Identify the specific components that make up an ADS system.
steps.retrievalText:
Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) uses several components to clear a fault safely. 

1. **Earthing Conductor**: The heavy-duty green and yellow cable connecting the Main Earth Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing (supplier terminal or electrode).

3. **Main Protective Bonding**: Conductors connecting metallic services (gas/water pipes) to the MET to ensure all metalwork stays at the same potential.
4. **Supplementary Bonding**: Local bonding in high-risk areas like bathrooms.
5. **Protective Device**: The MCB or fuse that actually disconnects the power when it detects high fault current flowing through the CPC.

Critical rules:
- Terms must be lesson-specific and grounded in the source.
- Definitions must be short and technically defensible.
- anchorPhrases should be reusable phrases that later phases can teach verbatim or near-verbatim.

terms.simpleDefinition: A system where the installation's earth connection is made through a local rod or plate driven into the ground.
terms.anchor: The 'Terra-Terra' system using a local earth electrode in the garden.
terms.misconception: Thinking it relies on the supplier's cable for an earth path; it actually relies entirely on the soil.

terms.simpleDefinition: A system where the supplier provides a completely separate earth conductor all the way back to the transformer.
terms.anchor: The 'Terre-Neutral-Separate' system with a dedicated earth wire from the street.
terms.misconception: Confusing it with TN-C-S; in TN-S, the earth and neutral are never the same wire.

terms.simpleDefinition: A system where the neutral and earth are combined in the supply cable but separated at the consumer's intake.
terms.misconception: Believing the earth and neutral stay combined inside the building; they must be separated at the main cut-out.

terms.simpleDefinition: The specific conductor that connects the exposed metal parts of a single circuit to the main earthing terminal.
terms.anchor: The 'earth wire' found inside a standard circuit cable like Twin and Earth.
terms.misconception: Calling this the 'Earthing Conductor'; the CPC is only for an individual circuit.

terms.simpleDefinition: The main conductor that connects the installation's Main Earth Terminal to the means of earthing (like the supplier's terminal or an electrode).
terms.anchor: The single heavy-duty green/yellow cable connecting the consumer unit to the earth source.
terms.misconception: Thinking every green/yellow wire is an earthing conductor; most are actually CPCs or bonding conductors.

terms.simpleDefinition: Conductors used to connect metal services (like gas or water pipes) to the main earthing terminal to prevent a voltage difference.
terms.anchor: The safety wires connected to the main incoming gas and water pipes.
terms.misconception: Thinking these carry fault current from appliances; they are actually there to keep all metalwork at the same potential.

terms.simpleDefinition: Additional earth connections between metal parts in high-risk areas, such as bathrooms, to reduce the risk of electric shock.
terms.anchor: Extra green/yellow links between pipes and metalwork in a shower room.
terms.misconception: Assuming these are required in every room; they are usually specific to locations with increased shock risk.

terms.simpleDefinition: A component like a circuit breaker or fuse designed to automatically cut off the power during a fault.
terms.anchor: The MCB or fuse that 'trips' or blows to stop the flow of electricity.
terms.misconception: Thinking the earth wire itself stops the fault; the earth wire just provides the path so the protective device can trip.

terms.simpleDefinition: A metal rod, plate, or mesh buried in the ground to provide a physical connection to the Earth.
terms.anchor: The copper-plated rod hammered into the earth for a TT system.
terms.misconception: Believing every house needs one; they are primarily used when the supplier does not provide an earth terminal.

anchorPhrases: Local earth rod for TT systems
Combined neutral and earth for TN-C-S
Bonding for gas and water pipes
misconceptionTargets: The difference between a CPC and the Earthing Conductor
The distinction between TN-S and TN-C-S supply arrangements
The requirement for an earth electrode in TT systems versus TN systems
The role of bonding versus the role of earthing

retrievalTextLines: Earthing systems are identified by a coding system where 'T' stands for Terre (Earth), 'N' for Neutral, 'S' for Separate, and 'C' for Combined. In a TT earthing system, the supplier provides no earth terminal. Instead, the installation relies on a local earth electrode, such as a copper-plated rod driven into the ground, to provide the connection to Earth. This means the 'Terra-Terra' arrangement uses the soil itself as the return path for fault current.

In a TN-S earthing system, the supplier provides a dedicated, separate earth conductor all the way from the transformer to the consumer's intake. This is often the lead sheath or a separate wire within the supply cable. The neutral and earth conductors are never connected together within the installation.

A TN-C-S earthing system uses a single conductor for both neutral and earth in the supplier's street cables (combined), but these are separated into two distinct terminals at the consumer's main cut-out. This arrangement is the most common for new domestic properties and is frequently referred to as Protective Multiple Earthing (PME).
teachChecks.hint: Focus on whether the earth and neutral are separate (S) or combined (C) in the supplier's cable, and remember that TT always requires a local rod.

teachChecks.title: ADS Component Parts
teachChecks.objective: Identify the specific components that make up an ADS system.
retrievalTextLines: Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) relies on several specific components working together to clear a fault safely. The earthing conductor is the heavy-duty green and yellow cable that connects the installation's Main Earth Terminal to the means of earthing, such as the supplier's terminal or an earth electrode. Do not confuse this with the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC), which is the individual earth wire found within each specific circuit, such as the central wire in a twin and earth cable.

Main protective bonding conductors are used to connect metallic services like incoming gas and water pipes to the main earth terminal. This ensures all accessible metalwork stays at the same potential, reducing shock risk. In high-risk areas like bathrooms, supplementary bonding may be used to link metal parts together locally. Finally, the protective device, such as an MCB or fuse, is the component that actually disconnects the power. It detects the high current flowing through the CPC during a fault and 'trips' or blows to stop the flow of electricity instantly.
teachChecks.hint: The earthing conductor connects the whole house to the source, while the CPC is only for one circuit. Bonding is for pipes, not circuits.

teachChecks.title: Earthing System Types

basicQuestions.questionText: In the coding system for earthing arrangements, what does the letter 'S' stand for in a TN-S system?
answerGuidance: The earth and neutral are separate conductors
Dedicated earth conductor from the transformer

basicQuestions.questionText: Which earthing system requires the installation of a local earth electrode because the supplier does not provide an earth terminal?
answerGuidance: Uses a copper-plated rod or plate in the ground

basicQuestions.questionText: In a TN-C-S system, where are the combined neutral and earth conductors from the street cable separated into two distinct terminals?
answerGuidance: Before entering the installation's main earthing terminal

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Explain the fundamental difference in the fault current return path between a TT system and a TN-S system.
deeperAnswerGuidance: TT relies on the soil or ground as the return path
TN-S uses a dedicated metal conductor provided by the supplier
TT uses a local earth electrode (rod)
TN-S has a continuous metallic path back to the transformer
teachChecks.hint: Think about whether the return path is a piece of wire provided by the supplier or the actual ground under your feet.

teachChecks.title: ADS Component Parts

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the specific name of the conductor found within an individual circuit cable that connects exposed metal parts to the main earthing terminal?
answerGuidance: The earth wire inside twin and earth cable

basicQuestions.questionText: Which component is used to connect metallic services, such as incoming gas and water pipes, to the main earth terminal?
answerGuidance: Ensures metalwork stays at the same potential

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the name of the heavy-duty green and yellow cable that connects the installation's Main Earth Terminal to the supplier's earth terminal or an electrode?
answerGuidance: The main connection to the means of earthing
Connects the MET to the source of earth

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: How do the CPC and the protective device (such as an MCB) work together to ensure safety during an electrical fault?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The CPC provides a low-resistance path for fault current
The high current flow is detected by the protective device
The protective device (MCB or fuse) trips or blows
This results in the automatic disconnection of the supply (ADS)
teachChecks.hint: One component provides the path for the electricity to flow, while the other component reacts to that flow to turn the power off.

      "retrievalTextLines": [

Instead, the installation relies on a local earth electrode, such as a copper-plated rod driven into the ground, to provide the connection to Earth. parsed.objective: Demonstrate how to identify the earthing system and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic installation using a PME (TN-C-S) supply.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are inspecting the service intake of a modern semi-detached house. You observe that the supplier's incoming cable enters a cut-out where the earth terminal is physically linked to the neutral block. From this point, a single green and yellow cable runs to the consumer unit's Main Earth Terminal (MET).

Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Look at the relationship between Neutral and Earth at the intake. Since they are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the cut-out into distinct terminals, this is a TN-C-S system (also known as PME).

Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. Locate the heavy-duty green and yellow cable connecting the supplier’s earth terminal to the MET. This is the Earthing Conductor, the primary link for the entire installation to the earth source.

Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). Look at the internal wiring of a socket circuit. The green and yellow wire within the twin and earth cable is the CPC. Its job is to carry fault current from that specific circuit back to the MET.

Step 4: Identify the Protective Devices. Look at the consumer unit. The MCBs (Miniature Circuit Breakers) are the protective devices. When a fault occurs and current flows through the CPC, the MCB detects the surge and trips.

Step 5: Identify Main Protective Bonding. Locate the green and yellow cables running to the incoming metallic gas and water pipes. These are the Main Protective Bonding conductors, required to keep all metalwork at the same potential.
Final Result: The installation is a TN-C-S system. The ADS components identified are the Earthing Conductor, CPCs, Protective Devices (MCBs), and Main Protective Bonding conductors.

Takeaway: ADS requires a complete path and a switching device. The CPC and Earthing Conductor provide the path for fault current, while the Protective Device (MCB) provides the automatic disconnection.
parsed.hint: Remember to distinguish between the conductor that serves the whole house (Earthing Conductor) and the one that serves just one circuit (CPC).

guidedPractice.objective: Identify the earthing system and ADS components for a rural installation using a local earth rod.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are working at a rural cottage. The electricity supplier has not provided an earth terminal. To provide an earth path, a copper-plated rod has been driven into the soil outside.
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the installation relies on a local connection to the earth (Terra) and the source is also earthed (Terra), this is a TT system.
Step 2: Identify the Earth Electrode. The copper-plated rod driven into the ground is the Earth Electrode.
Step 3: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The green and yellow cable connecting the Earth Electrode to the Main Earth Terminal (MET) is the Earthing Conductor.

Step 4: Identify the Protective Device. In a TT system, an RCD is typically used as the protective device to ensure automatic disconnection because the earth path through the soil has higher resistance.

      "objective": "Identify and distinguish between TT, TN-S, and TN-C-S earthing systems.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Earthing systems are identified by a coding system where 'T' stands for Terre (Earth), 'N' for Neutral, 'S' for Separate, and 'C' for Combined. In a TT earthing system, the supplier provides no earth terminal. Instead, the installation relies on a local earth electrode, such as a copper-plated rod driven into the ground, to provide the connection to Earth. This means the 'Terra-Terra' arrangement uses the soil itself as the return path for fault current.",
        "In a TN-S earthing system, the supplier provides a dedicated, separate earth conductor all the way from the transformer to the consumer's intake. This is often the lead sheath or a separate wire within the supply cable. The neutral and earth conductors are never connected together within the installation.",
        "A TN-C-S earthing system uses a single conductor for both neutral and earth in the supplier's street cables (combined), but these are separated into two distinct terminals at the consumer's main cut-out. This arrangement is the most common for new domestic properties and is frequently referred to as Protective Multiple Earthing (PME)."
      ],
      "hint": "Focus on whether the earth and neutral are separate (S) or combined (C) in the supplier's cable, and remember that TT always requires a local rod."
    },

      "objective": "Identify the specific components that make up an ADS system.",
      "retrievalTextLines": [
        "Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) relies on several specific components working together to clear a fault safely. The earthing conductor is the heavy-duty green and yellow cable that connects the installation's Main Earth Terminal to the means of earthing, such as the supplier's terminal or an earth electrode. Do not confuse this with the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC), which is the individual earth wire found within each specific circuit, such as the central wire in a twin and earth cable.",
        "Main protective bonding conductors are used to connect metallic services like incoming gas and water pipes to the main earth terminal. This ensures all accessible metalwork stays at the same potential, reducing shock risk. In high-risk areas like bathrooms, supplementary bonding may be used to link metal parts together locally. Finally, the protective device, such as an MCB or fuse, is the component that actually disconnects the power. It detects the high current flowing through the CPC during a fault and 'trips' or blows to stop the flow of electricity instantly."

retrievalTextLines: To master earthing and ADS, you must be able to look at a supply arrangement and immediately know which components are required to make it safe. In a TN-C-S (PME) system, the supply arrives with a combined neutral and earth, but the internal installation must treat them as separate. Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is only achieved when the correct conductors—the Earthing Conductor, the CPC, and the Main Protective Bonding—are all installed to provide a low-resistance path for fault current and to equalize potential across metal services.
integrative.questionText: You are installing a new consumer unit in a property with a TN-C-S (PME) supply. To ensure the ADS system is compliant, you must connect the installation to the supplier's terminal, wire a new lighting circuit, and secure the incoming metallic water main. Identify the earthing system type and name the three specific green-and-yellow conductors required for these three distinct tasks.
answerGuidance: Earthing Conductor (to connect the MET to the supplier's terminal)
Circuit Protective Conductor / CPC (for the lighting circuit)
Main Protective Bonding Conductor (for the metallic water main)
integrative.hint: Think about the specific names we use for conductors based on their job: one connects the whole house to the source, one protects an individual circuit, and one 'bonds' the metal pipes.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To identify the three primary earthing system types (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
steps.retrievalText:
Lesson: Earthing Systems and ADS Components
To identify the three primary earthing system types (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
By the end of this lesson you should be able to:
1. TT earthing system
2. TN-S earthing system
3. TN-C-S earthing system
We will work in short chunks: teaching first, then checks, then example, practice, and a final integrative task.

steps.title: Earthing System Types
steps.objective: Identify and distinguish between TT, TN-S, and TN-C-S earthing systems.
steps.retrievalText:
Earthing systems are identified by a coding system where 'T' stands for Terre (Earth), 'N' for Neutral, 'S' for Separate, and 'C' for Combined. In a TT earthing system, the supplier provides no earth terminal. Instead, the installation relies on a local earth electrode, such as a copper-plated rod driven into the ground, to provide the connection to Earth. This means the 'Terra-Terra' arrangement uses the soil itself as the return path for fault current.
In a TN-S earthing system, the supplier provides a dedicated, separate earth conductor all the way from the transformer to the consumer's intake. This is often the lead sheath or a separate wire within the supply cable. The neutral and earth conductors are never connected together within the installation.
A TN-C-S earthing system uses a single conductor for both neutral and earth in the supplier's street cables (combined), but these are separated into two distinct terminals at the consumer's main cut-out. This arrangement is the most common for new domestic properties and is frequently referred to as Protective Multiple Earthing (PME).

basicQuestions.questionText: In the coding system for earthing arrangements, what does the letter 'S' stand for in a TN-S system?
answerGuidance: The earth and neutral are separate conductors
Dedicated earth conductor from the transformer

basicQuestions.questionText: Which earthing system requires the installation of a local earth electrode because the supplier does not provide an earth terminal?
answerGuidance: Uses a copper-plated rod or plate in the ground

basicQuestions.questionText: In a TN-C-S system, where are the combined neutral and earth conductors from the street cable separated into two distinct terminals?
answerGuidance: Before entering the installation's main earthing terminal

steps.questionText:
1. In the coding system for earthing arrangements, what does the letter 'S' stand for in a TN-S system?
2. Which earthing system requires the installation of a local earth electrode because the supplier does not provide an earth terminal?
3. In a TN-C-S system, where are the combined neutral and earth conductors from the street cable separated into two distinct terminals?
answerGuidance: The earth and neutral are separate conductors
Dedicated earth conductor from the transformer
Uses a copper-plated rod or plate in the ground
Before entering the installation's main earthing terminal
steps.deeperQuestionText: Explain the fundamental difference in the fault current return path between a TT system and a TN-S system.
deeperAnswerGuidance: TT relies on the soil or ground as the return path
TN-S uses a dedicated metal conductor provided by the supplier
TT uses a local earth electrode (rod)
TN-S has a continuous metallic path back to the transformer
steps.hint: Focus on whether the earth and neutral are separate (S) or combined (C) in the supplier's cable, and remember that TT always requires a local rod.

steps.title: ADS Component Parts
steps.objective: Identify the specific components that make up an ADS system.
steps.retrievalText:
Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) relies on several specific components working together to clear a fault safely. The earthing conductor is the heavy-duty green and yellow cable that connects the installation's Main Earth Terminal to the means of earthing, such as the supplier's terminal or an earth electrode. Do not confuse this with the Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC), which is the individual earth wire found within each specific circuit, such as the central wire in a twin and earth cable.
Main protective bonding conductors are used to connect metallic services like incoming gas and water pipes to the main earth terminal. This ensures all accessible metalwork stays at the same potential, reducing shock risk. In high-risk areas like bathrooms, supplementary bonding may be used to link metal parts together locally. Finally, the protective device, such as an MCB or fuse, is the component that actually disconnects the power. It detects the high current flowing through the CPC during a fault and 'trips' or blows to stop the flow of electricity instantly.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the specific name of the conductor found within an individual circuit cable that connects exposed metal parts to the main earthing terminal?
answerGuidance: The earth wire inside twin and earth cable

basicQuestions.questionText: Which component is used to connect metallic services, such as incoming gas and water pipes, to the main earth terminal?
answerGuidance: Ensures metalwork stays at the same potential

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the name of the heavy-duty green and yellow cable that connects the installation's Main Earth Terminal to the supplier's earth terminal or an electrode?
answerGuidance: The main connection to the means of earthing
Connects the MET to the source of earth

steps.questionText:

2. Which component is used to connect metallic services, such as incoming gas and water pipes, to the main earth terminal?
3. What is the name of the heavy-duty green and yellow cable that connects the installation's Main Earth Terminal to the supplier's earth terminal or an electrode?
answerGuidance: The earth wire inside twin and earth cable
Ensures metalwork stays at the same potential
The main connection to the means of earthing
Connects the MET to the source of earth
steps.deeperQuestionText: How do the CPC and the protective device (such as an MCB) work together to ensure safety during an electrical fault?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The CPC provides a low-resistance path for fault current
The high current flow is detected by the protective device
The protective device (MCB or fuse) trips or blows
This results in the automatic disconnection of the supply (ADS)
steps.hint: The earthing conductor connects the whole house to the source, while the CPC is only for one circuit. Bonding is for pipes, not circuits.

steps.title: Worked Example
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to identify the earthing system and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic installation using a PME (TN-C-S) supply.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are inspecting the service intake of a modern semi-detached house. You observe that the supplier's incoming cable enters a cut-out where the earth terminal is physically linked to the neutral block. From this point, a single green and yellow cable runs to the consumer unit's Main Earth Terminal (MET).
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Look at the relationship between Neutral and Earth at the intake. Since they are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the cut-out into distinct terminals, this is a TN-C-S system (also known as PME).
Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. Locate the heavy-duty green and yellow cable connecting the supplier’s earth terminal to the MET. This is the Earthing Conductor, the primary link for the entire installation to the earth source.
Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). Look at the internal wiring of a socket circuit. The green and yellow wire within the twin and earth cable is the CPC. Its job is to carry fault current from that specific circuit back to the MET.
Step 4: Identify the Protective Devices. Look at the consumer unit. The MCBs (Miniature Circuit Breakers) are the protective devices. When a fault occurs and current flows through the CPC, the MCB detects the surge and trips.
Step 5: Identify Main Protective Bonding. Locate the green and yellow cables running to the incoming metallic gas and water pipes. These are the Main Protective Bonding conductors, required to keep all metalwork at the same potential.
Final Result: The installation is a TN-C-S system. The ADS components identified are the Earthing Conductor, CPCs, Protective Devices (MCBs), and Main Protective Bonding conductors.
Takeaway: ADS requires a complete path and a switching device. The CPC and Earthing Conductor provide the path for fault current, while the Protective Device (MCB) provides the automatic disconnection.
steps.hint: Remember to distinguish between the conductor that serves the whole house (Earthing Conductor) and the one that serves just one circuit (CPC).

steps.title: Guided Practice
steps.objective: Identify the earthing system and ADS components for a rural installation using a local earth rod.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are working at a rural cottage. The electricity supplier has not provided an earth terminal. To provide an earth path, a copper-plated rod has been driven into the soil outside.
Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the installation relies on a local connection to the earth (Terra) and the source is also earthed (Terra), this is a TT system.
Step 2: Identify the Earth Electrode. The copper-plated rod driven into the ground is the Earth Electrode.
Step 3: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The green and yellow cable connecting the Earth Electrode to the Main Earth Terminal (MET) is the Earthing Conductor.
Step 4: Identify the Protective Device. In a TT system, an RCD is typically used as the protective device to ensure automatic disconnection because the earth path through the soil has higher resistance.
steps.questionText: Based on the scenario, name the earthing system type, the component driven into the ground, and the specific conductor that links that component to the consumer unit.
steps.hint: In a TT system, the 'T' stands for 'Terre' (Earth), meaning both the source and the installation have their own independent connections to the ground.

steps.title: Practice
steps.objective: Correctly identify a TN-S system and its specific ADS components from a technical description.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are inspecting a large Victorian house. The incoming supply cable has a dedicated metal sheath that acts as the earth return path. This earth path is kept entirely separate from the neutral conductor from the transformer all the way to the consumer's installation.
Task: You must identify the system type and the specific conductors used for a single shower circuit within this installation.
steps.questionText: 1. Identify the earthing system type described. 2. Name the conductor that connects the metal casing of the shower unit to the consumer unit's earth bar. 3. Name the conductor that connects the consumer unit's earth bar to the supplier's dedicated earth terminal.
steps.hint: Think about the 'S' in the acronym—it stands for 'Separate'. Also, distinguish between the conductor for a single circuit and the conductor for the whole installation.

steps.title: Integrative Close
steps.objective: Synthesize the identification of a TN-C-S system with the correct application of specific ADS conductors for circuits and services.
steps.retrievalText: To master earthing and ADS, you must be able to look at a supply arrangement and immediately know which components are required to make it safe. In a TN-C-S (PME) system, the supply arrives with a combined neutral and earth, but the internal installation must treat them as separate. Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is only achieved when the correct conductors—the Earthing Conductor, the CPC, and the Main Protective Bonding—are all installed to provide a low-resistance path for fault current and to equalize potential across metal services.
steps.questionText: You are installing a new consumer unit in a property with a TN-C-S (PME) supply. To ensure the ADS system is compliant, you must connect the installation to the supplier's terminal, wire a new lighting circuit, and secure the incoming metallic water main. Identify the earthing system type and name the three specific green-and-yellow conductors required for these three distinct tasks.
answerGuidance: Earthing Conductor (to connect the MET to the supplier's terminal)
Circuit Protective Conductor / CPC (for the lighting circuit)
Main Protective Bonding Conductor (for the metallic water main)
steps.hint: Think about the specific names we use for conductors based on their job: one connects the whole house to the source, one protects an individual circuit, and one 'bonds' the metal pipes.

steps.title: Intro
steps.objective: To identify the three primary earthing system types (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and the specific components required for Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS).
steps.retrievalText: Every electrical installation needs a safety net. If a fault occurs and a metal appliance becomes live, the earthing system provides a safe path for that current, and the ADS (Automatic Disconnection of Supply) acts as the emergency off-switch. Today we will learn how to identify the three main types of earthing systems (TT, TN-S, TN-C-S) and the components that make this safety net work. By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to identify these systems and the specific conductors required to keep an installation safe. We will work in short chunks: teaching first, then checks, then a worked example, practice, and a final integrative task.

steps.title: Earthing System Types
steps.objective: Identify and distinguish between TT, TN-S, and TN-C-S earthing systems.
steps.retrievalText:
Earthing systems are identified by a coding system: 'T' stands for Terre (Earth), 'N' for Neutral, 'S' for Separate, and 'C' for Combined. 

In a **TT** system, the supplier provides no earth terminal. The installation relies on a local earth electrode (like a copper rod) driven into the ground. The soil itself is the return path.

In a **TN-S** system, the supplier provides a dedicated, separate earth conductor from the transformer to the consumer. This is often the lead sheath of the supply cable. Neutral and earth are separate throughout.

In a **TN-C-S** system, the supplier uses one conductor for both neutral and earth in the street cables (combined), but they are separated into two distinct terminals at the consumer's main cut-out. This is the most common domestic arrangement, often called PME (Protective Multiple Earthing).

steps.retrievalText:
Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) uses several components to clear a fault safely. 

1. **Earthing Conductor**: The heavy-duty green and yellow cable connecting the Main Earth Terminal (MET) to the means of earthing (supplier terminal or electrode).
2. **Circuit Protective Conductor (CPC)**: The individual earth wire within each circuit (e.g., the bare wire in twin and earth cable).
3. **Main Protective Bonding**: Conductors connecting metallic services (gas/water pipes) to the MET to ensure all metalwork stays at the same potential.
4. **Supplementary Bonding**: Local bonding in high-risk areas like bathrooms.
5. **Protective Device**: The MCB or fuse that actually disconnects the power when it detects high fault current flowing through the CPC.

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the specific name of the conductor found within an individual circuit cable that connects exposed metal parts to the main earthing terminal?
answerGuidance: The earth wire inside twin and earth cable

basicQuestions.questionText: Which component is used to connect metallic services, such as incoming gas and water pipes, to the main earth terminal?
What is the name of the heavy-duty green and yellow cable that connects the installation's Main Earth Terminal to the supplier's earth terminal or an electrode?
answerGuidance: The main connection to the means of earthing
Connects the MET to the source of earth

What is the name of the heavy-duty green and yellow cable that connects the installation's Main Earth Terminal to the supplier's earth terminal or an electrode?
answerGuidance: The earth wire inside twin and earth cable
The main connection to the means of earthing
Connects the MET to the source of earth
steps.deeperQuestionText: How do the CPC and the protective device (such as an MCB) work together to ensure safety during an electrical fault?
deeperAnswerGuidance: The CPC provides a low-resistance path for fault current
The high current flow is detected by the protective device
The protective device (MCB or fuse) trips or blows
This results in the automatic disconnection of the supply (ADS)
steps.hint: The earthing conductor connects the whole house to the source, while the CPC is only for one circuit. Bonding is for pipes, not circuits.

steps.title: Worked Example
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to identify the earthing system and list the required ADS components for a standard domestic installation using a PME (TN-C-S) supply.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are inspecting the service intake of a modern semi-detached house. You observe that the supplier's incoming cable enters a cut-out where the earth terminal is physically linked to the neutral block. From this point, a single green and yellow cable runs to the consumer unit's Main Earth Terminal (MET).

Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Look at the relationship between Neutral and Earth at the intake. Since they are combined in the supplier's cable but separated at the cut-out into distinct terminals, this is a TN-C-S system (PME).

Step 2: Identify the Earthing Conductor. Locate the heavy-duty green and yellow cable connecting the supplier’s earth terminal to the MET. This is the Earthing Conductor.

Step 3: Identify the Circuit Protective Conductors (CPCs). Look at the internal wiring of a socket circuit. The green and yellow wire within the twin and earth cable is the CPC.

Step 4: Identify the Protective Devices. Look at the consumer unit. The MCBs (Miniature Circuit Breakers) are the protective devices that provide the 'disconnection' in ADS.

Step 5: Identify Main Protective Bonding. Locate the green and yellow cables running to the incoming metallic gas and water pipes. These are the Main Protective Bonding conductors.

Final Result: The installation is a TN-C-S system. The ADS components identified are the Earthing Conductor, CPCs, Protective Devices (MCBs), and Main Protective Bonding conductors.

Takeaway: ADS requires a complete path (conductors) and a switching device (MCB).
steps.hint: Remember to distinguish between the conductor that serves the whole house (Earthing Conductor) and the one that serves just one circuit (CPC).

steps.title: Guided Practice
steps.objective: Identify the earthing system and ADS components for a rural installation using a local earth rod.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are working at a rural cottage. The electricity supplier has not provided an earth terminal. To provide an earth path, a copper-plated rod has been driven into the soil outside.

Step 1: Identify the Earthing System. Because the installation relies on a local connection to the earth (Terra) and the source is also earthed (Terra), this is a TT system.

Step 2: Identify the Earth Electrode. The copper-plated rod driven into the ground is the Earth Electrode.

Step 3: Identify the Earthing Conductor. The green and yellow cable connecting the Earth Electrode to the Main Earth Terminal (MET) is the Earthing Conductor.

Step 4: Identify the Protective Device. In a TT system, an RCD is typically used as the protective device to ensure automatic disconnection because the earth path through the soil has higher resistance than a metal cable.
steps.questionText: Based on the scenario, name the earthing system type, the component driven into the ground, and the specific conductor that links that component to the consumer unit.
steps.hint: In a TT system, the 'T' stands for 'Terre' (Earth), meaning both the source and the installation have their own independent connections to the ground.

steps.title: Practice
steps.objective: Correctly identify a TN-S system and its specific ADS components from a technical description.
steps.retrievalText:
Scenario: You are inspecting a large Victorian house. The incoming supply cable has a dedicated metal sheath that acts as the earth return path. This earth path is kept entirely separate from the neutral conductor from the transformer all the way to the consumer's installation.

Task: You must identify the system type and the specific conductors used for a single shower circuit within this installation.
steps.questionText: 1. Identify the earthing system type described. 2. Name the conductor that connects the metal casing of the shower unit to the consumer unit's earth bar. 3. Name the conductor that connects the consumer unit's earth bar to the supplier's dedicated earth terminal.
steps.hint: Think about the 'S' in the acronym—it stands for 'Separate'. Also, distinguish between the conductor for a single circuit and the conductor for the whole installation.

steps.title: Integrative Close
steps.objective: Synthesize the identification of a TN-C-S system with the correct application of specific ADS conductors for circuits and services.
steps.retrievalText: To master earthing and ADS, you must be able to look at a supply arrangement and immediately know which components are required to make it safe. In a TN-C-S (PME) system, the supply arrives with a combined neutral and earth, but the internal installation must treat them as separate. Automatic Disconnection of Supply (ADS) is only achieved when the correct conductors—the Earthing Conductor, the CPC, and the Main Protective Bonding—are all installed to provide a low-resistance path for fault current and to equalize potential across metal services.
steps.questionText: You are installing a new consumer unit in a property with a TN-C-S (PME) supply. To ensure the ADS system is compliant, you must connect the installation to the supplier's terminal, wire a new lighting circuit, and secure the incoming metallic water main. Identify the earthing system type and name the three specific green-and-yellow conductors required for these three distinct tasks.
steps.hint: Think about the specific names we use for conductors based on their job: one connects the whole house to the source, one protects an individual circuit, and one 'bonds' the metal pipes.
